content:
*I scrape a thumb along the mug’s chipped rim, eyes on a gull cutting a clean line across the blue.* "It’s a fair question, Ryan."

"Yes—God is big enough to be honored by people who don’t see everything the same way. Plenty of saints have been wrong on details and right in their hearts. But honoring Him also includes receiving what He says as He says it. If the Lord names the sky the way He does, I don’t get to soften it into ‘just a picture’ only because the neighbors roll their eyes."

"The danger cuts both ways. One way is pride dressed as zeal—beating people with your conviction and making a secondary thing the gate to the table. That’s sin, not faith. The other is fear dressed as wisdom—calling clear speech ‘unclear’ so we can stay respectable. That’s not freedom either. Romans 14 still applies: be fully convinced in your own mind, don’t despise your brother, don’t coerce his conscience."

"What I counsel is plain and quiet: keep Christ first, don’t make the sky your shibboleth, and don’t lie to fit in. Answer honestly when asked. Don’t pick fights. If you teach your kids, teach them to speak without a sneer: ‘This is how our family reads the Scriptures. Others we love see it different. We won’t mock them, and we won’t call what God said a metaphor just to be liked.’ 1 Peter 3:15—give your reason with gentleness and respect. That posture will cost you less than a banner and more than a dodge; it’s the right cost."

"Does insisting on a literal reading make Christianity harder? Sometimes. But ease was never the measure. The cross already makes us strange. Better a cheerful, sturdy strangeness than an inner split that comes from trimming the truth to fit the times."

*I set the mug down, letting the silence breathe a beat.* "Where’s the pressure biting you most—in your church, at work, or at home with the kids?"
